did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used AI/ML/DL methods for skin cancer diagnosis. Specifically, it implemented a multi-level classification system using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Deep Neural Networks (DNN) for deep learning, various machine learning classifiers, and linear regression classifiers. These models were applied to dermoscopy images to classify pigmented skin lesions into benign/malignant categories and further into specific cancer types (basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, melanoma).

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the design and implementation of a multi-level classification system for skin cancer diagnosis using CNN, DNN, and linear regression models on the ISIC Archive dataset. It reports specific experimental results, including accuracy metrics (e.g., 98% and 90% for the linear regression classifier), and describes the methodology, database, and algorithm implementation, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Skin Cancer Diagnosis With Multi-Level Classification
brief_summary: This study presents a multi-level classification system for diagnosing pigmented skin lesions and melanoma using dermoscopy images from the ISIC Archive dataset. The system employs a two-stage approach: first classifying lesions as benign or malignant, and then identifying specific cancer types (basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, or melanoma) for malignant cases. Three modeling approaches were evaluated: a deep learning classifier (CNN/DNN), a machine learning classifier, and a linear regression classifier utilizing Histograms of Oriented Gradients (HOG) features. The linear regression model achieved the highest performance, with 98% accuracy in the first stage and 90% in the second stage. The results highlight the critical role of feature selection in enhancing classification accuracy for skin cancer diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: Classified in the first stage of the multi-level classification system as one of two categories (benign vs. malignant).
Classified in the second stage of the system for malignant cases. Identified as one of the three primary categories of skin cancer.
Classified in the second stage of the system for malignant cases. Identified as one of the three primary categories of skin cancer.
Classified in the second stage of the system for malignant cases. Identified as one of the three primary categories of skin cancer and the most lethal form.
Mentioned in the context of dermoscopy diagnosis of melanocytic lesions ('nevus and melanoma') but not explicitly listed as a distinct target class in the experimental classification stages.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories: benign vs. malignant (binary) and specific skin cancer types (basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, melanoma) (multiclass).
all_task_types.details: The paper implements a multi-level classification system. Stage 1 classifies pigmented skin lesions into two discrete categories: benign or malignant. Stage 2 classifies malignant lesions into one of three discrete classes: basal cell carcinomas, squamous cell carcinomas, or melanoma. The study evaluates three modeling approaches (deep learning/CNN-DNN, machine learning, and linear regression classifier) all used for this classification task, with performance measured by accuracy percentages (e.g., 98% and 90% for the linear regression classifier).

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive
datasets_used.description: Dermoscopic images obtained from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC Archive). The dataset contains 1200 images of pigmented skin lesions, including benign and malignant cases (basal cell carcinomas, squamous cell carcinomas, and melanoma).

use.training: 80% of the dataset (960 images) was used for training the models.
use.testing: 20% of the dataset (240 images) was used to evaluate the models' performance and calculate accuracy.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of the classification models (deep learning, machine learning, and linear regression) in both the first stage (benign vs. malignant) and the second stage (specific cancer types).
Used in linear regression to produce a line of best fit for the relationship between dependent and independent variables.
Used in logistic regression to determine the parameters of the model that best fit the data.
To extract texture features (contrast, correlation, energy, homogeneity, entropy, variance) from dermoscopy images for machine learning classification.
To extract features by counting gradient orientation in localized portions of an image for the linear regression classifier.
To identify the most influential features (combinations of texture, shape, and color metrics) for each classifier to enhance accuracy.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy metrics on a held-out test set.

First Approach (Deep Learning Classifier.description: CNN/DNN architecture with automatic feature extraction
First Approach (Deep Learning Classifier.notes: The paper notes that the result in the second stage is 'very bad' and the algorithm is suitable for classification between two classes.

Second Approach (Machine Learning Classi.description: Uses GLCM features (contrast, correlation, energy, homogeneity) and Hu's moments, followed by DCT transformation

Third Approach (Linear Regression Classi.description: Uses Histograms of Oriented Gradients (HOG) features with a multiple linear regression classifier
Third Approach (Linear Regression Classi.notes: Identified as the most effective model. The paper emphasizes that feature selection significantly impacts performance.

architecture_used.main_model: Three approaches were evaluated: 1) Deep Learning Classifier (CNN/DNN), 2) Machine Learning Classifier (using GLCM features and Hu's moments with DCT), 3) Linear Regression Classifier (using HOG features).

deep_learning_classifier.activation_function: ReLU for convolutional/pooling layers; Softmax for fully connected layer
deep_learning_classifier.notes: The paper notes the deep learning result in the second stage was 'very bad' and suitable mainly for binary classification.

feature_extraction: Gray-level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) features (contrast, correlation, energy, homogeneity)
Discrete Cosine Transformation (DCT) applied to 8x8 pixel blocks

classification_or_output_head.deep_learning: Softmax activation function for probability output
classification_or_output_head.linear_regression: Linear regression model applied to HOG features

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Machine Learning Classifier (using Gray-level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) features and Hu's moments)
Linear Regression Classifier (using Histograms of Oriented Gradients (HOG) features)
preprocessing_techniques: Conversion of input images to grayscale
Exclusion of non-image features (gender, age, location of skin disease) from the dataset
Utilization of dermoscopy images which inherently provide higher contrast and eliminate the need for additional image enhancement filters
conclusions: The study developed a multi-level classification system for skin cancer diagnosis that first distinguishes between benign and malignant lesions and then identifies specific cancer types (basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, or melanoma).

Among the three evaluated approaches (deep learning, machine learning, and linear regression), the linear regression classifier utilizing Histograms of Oriented Gradients (HOG) features achieved the highest performance.
The linear regression model attained an accuracy of 98% in the first stage (benign vs. malignant classification) and 90% in the second stage (specific cancer type classification).

The deep learning classifier (CNN/DNN) achieved 86.08% accuracy in the first stage and 79.12% in the second stage, but the authors noted its performance in the second stage was 'very bad' and it was more suitable for binary classification.
The machine learning classifier achieved 74.63% accuracy in the first stage and 64.41% in the second stage.
Feature selection plays a critical role in influencing model accuracy, with the optimal selection of features leading to the superior performance of the linear regression model.
The proposed system aims to reduce misdiagnoses and assist physicians by providing a cost-effective and precise method for visual interpretation of dermoscopy images.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small, single dataset (ISIC Archive) of only 1200 images, which limits the generalizability and statistical power of the findings.
No external validation was performed; the models were evaluated solely on a held-out 20% test split from the same dataset, raising concerns about overfitting or dataset-specific bias.

The paper explicitly notes that excluding clinical metadata (gender, age, lesion location) reduced accuracy, suggesting the models may not be robust to real-world clinical scenarios where such data is typically available.

The deep learning approach (CNN/DNN) was self-assessed by the authors as having 'very bad' performance in the second stage (79.12% accuracy) and deemed suitable only for binary classification, indicating a significant weakness in the deep learning component.

The best-performing model (Linear Regression with HOG features) achieved high accuracy (98% Stage 1, 90% Stage 2), but the use of linear regression for image classification is unconventional compared to standard deep learning or SVM approaches, and its sensitivity to outliers is noted in the methodology.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was reported to validate the significance of the performance differences between the three models.

The study does not report on data leakage risks, although the exclusion of non-image features (age, gender) suggests an attempt to prevent leakage from clinical metadata, the potential for leakage within the image data itself (e.g., patient ID artifacts) is not addressed.
The dataset class distribution is not provided, making it impossible to assess potential class imbalance issues, which are common in skin cancer datasets and can skew accuracy metrics.
